initial_draft:
<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is offering 40 ZUP, which is exactly my production cost. This means I would break even with zero profit. While I need to sell the object, I have one more proposal turn available before I must accept or reject. It is strategically better to hold out for at least one ZUP of profit rather than accepting a deal that yields nothing. I will counter-offer with 41 ZUP to secure a small profit margin. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 41 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Player BLUE, 40 ZUP covers my costs, but I cannot sell at a loss or break even. I need to make at least a 1 ZUP profit. Let's try 41 ZUP for the X.</message>

iterations.refined:
<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE explicitly stated they are only willing to pay "exactly 40 ZUP". Proposing 41 ZUP is futile as it exceeds their stated maximum valuation, guaranteeing a rejection. Since I have used 2 out of my 3 allowed proposals, wasting the final proposal on an impossible price is risky. If I propose 41 and get rejected, the game might end or I might be forced into a suboptimal position with no proposals left. The safest strategic move to ensure the trade completes (even at break-even) is to accept their offer of 40 ZUP immediately. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> Player BLUE, since you have indicated 40 ZUP is your maximum, I accept that price to finalize the trade. We are done.</message>
